title: New U.S. Citizens | USCIS
content:
Letter from the President
Congratulations on becoming a U.S. citizen! A congratulatory letter from the President of the United States is available for viewing and printing.
Naturalization Survey
Have you completed your citizenship ceremony in the last 2 days? We would appreciate hearing about your experience. Take our survey to tell us about it—it takes less than 5 minutes.
Congratulations new U.S. citizens! As a citizen of this great nation, you now hold important rights and responsibilities. To support our new citizens, we have compiled valuable resources and information for U.S. citizens. We also invite you to celebrate your citizenship by sharing your story with us on X (formerly Twitter ), Instagram , or Facebook , using @USCIS and #NewUSCitizen.
We also encourage you to read The Citizen's Almanac , which contains information on the history, people, and events that have shaped our country and the important responsibilities and rights of citizenship. If you are not a U.S. citizen and would like to learn more about becoming one, please visit the Citizenship Resource Center .

#### Apply for a U.S. Passport
As a U.S. citizen, you are now eligible for a U.S. passport from the U.S. Department of State. In addition to your Certificate of Naturalization, a U.S. passport serves as official proof of citizenship. When applying for your U.S. passport, you will need to submit your original Certificate of Naturalization AND a photocopy. Visit the Department of State's website for more information about:
- Applying for your U.S. passport.
- Where to  submit your passport application.
You may also apply for a U.S. passport for any child under the age of 18 who is eligible to automatically acquire citizenship based on your naturalization.

#### Update your Record with the Social Security Administration
It is important that your Social Security record is accurate because you will need your Social Security number to get a job, collect Social Security benefits, and receive other government services. You will need to contact the Social Security Administration (SSA) to update your records to indicate that you are a U.S. citizen. When you are hired for a job, your employer can enter your Social Security number into a U.S. Department of Homeland Security web-based system, E-Verify , to determine your eligibility to work in the United States. If your record has not been updated, this may impact the information your employer receives about your work eligibility. If you have any questions about your Social Security card, or would like to find your Social Security office, call 1-800-772-1213 or visit ssa.gov .

#### Obtain a Certificate of Citizenship for Your Child
If you have a child who is a lawful permanent resident under the age of 18 on the day you naturalize, they may have automatically derived U.S. citizenship. For more information, see " I am the child of a U.S. Citizen ." To obtain evidence of your child’s derivative U.S. citizenship status, file Form N-600, Application for Certificate of Citizenship, online. You can find instructions and filing information online at uscis.gov/forms . You can also apply for a U.S. passport for your child from the U.S. Department of State.

#### Sponsor Family Members to Come to the United States
As a U.S. citizen, you may petition for certain relatives to become lawful permanent residents in the United States and obtain what is often referred to as a “Green Card.” To do so, you need to sponsor your relative and be able to prove that you have enough income or assets to support your relative(s) in the United States. For more information, see Family of U.S. Citizens .

#### Update Your Record with the Agency that Issues Driver’s Licenses in Your State
If you have a state-issued driver’s license or identification card (ID) and you change your name or your place of residence, you should update your record with the agency that issues driver’s licenses in your state, often called a Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV). To update your address or legal name on a DMV-issued ID card, contact the DMV agency in your state. You can search for your state DMV using USA.gov .
Voting in elections is a responsibility and a right that comes with U.S. citizenship. Before you can vote, you need to register. Go to vote.gov to find out how to register in your state. You can fill out a voter registration form online, by mail, or in person. You can also register when you apply for or renew your driver’s license.
You can also find additional information about voting in the Voter’s Guide to Federal Elections .

#### Replace Your Certificate of Naturalization
If you lose your Certificate of Naturalization, you may replace it by filing Form N-565, Application for Replacement Naturalization/Citizenship Document, online . You can find instructions and filing fee information at uscis.gov/N-565 . You may use your U.S. passport as evidence of citizenship until you receive your replacement certificate.
The U.S. Small Business Administration (SBA) is the nation’s go-to resource and voice for small businesses. The SBA provides free or low-cost counseling, capital, and contracting expertise. The SBA brochure outlines SBA’s services, how to find local assistance through its district offices and resource partners, and where to go for more information.
